quiz.title: SAT Mathematics: Unit 9 Quiz 5
quiz.subtitle: Measures of Center and Spread
quiz.subject: SAT Preparation
quiz.topic: Mean, median, range, and standard deviation

questions.question: A set of 7 integers has a mean of $12$. If a new integer, $28$, is added to the set, what is the mean of the new set of 8 integers?
questions.explanation: First, find the sum of the original 7 integers: $7 \times 12 = 84$. Add the new integer: $84 + 28 = 112$. Divide the new sum by the new total number of integers: $112 / 8 = 14$.

questions.question:
The table below shows two data sets.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|}\hline \text{Data Set A} & \text{Data Set B} \\ \hline 10 & 10 \\ \hline 20 & 25 \\ \hline 30 & 30 \\ \hline 40 & 35 \\ \hline 50 & 50 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which of the following statements is true regarding the standard deviations of Data Set A and Data Set B?
options: The standard deviation of Data Set A is greater than Data Set B.
The standard deviation of Data Set B is greater than Data Set A.
The standard deviations of both sets are equal.
The standard deviation cannot be compared without the mean.
questions.explanation: Standard deviation measures how far data points are from the mean. Both sets have a mean of 30. In Set A, the values (20 and 40) are further from the mean than the corresponding values in Set B (25 and 35). Because Set A's data is more spread out, its standard deviation is greater.

questions.question: A list of numbers consists of: $5, 8, 12, 12, 15, 19, 21$. If the number $21$ is replaced with the number $35$, which of the following measures will change the most?
questions.explanation: The original range is $21 - 5 = 16$. The new range is $35 - 5 = 30$, an increase of 14. The mean increases by only $14 / 7 = 2$. The median (12) and mode (12) do not change at all. Thus, the range changes the most.

questions.question: The median of a set of 11 distinct numbers is $45$. If the 3 largest numbers in the set are increased by 10 each, what is the median of the new set?
questions.explanation: The median is the middle value. In a set of 11 numbers, the median is the 6th value when ordered. Increasing the 9th, 10th, and 11th values does not change the position or the value of the 6th number. Therefore, the median remains 45.

questions.question:
The table below shows the frequency of scores on a 5-point quiz for 20 students.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|}\hline \text{Score} & \text{Frequency} \\ \hline 1 & 2 \\ \hline 2 & 3 \\ \hline 3 & 5 \\ \hline 4 & 7 \\ \hline 5 & 3 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

What is the mean score for the 20 students?
questions.explanation: Calculate the total points: $(1\times2) + (2\times3) + (3\times5) + (4\times7) + (5\times3) = 2 + 6 + 15 + 28 + 15 = 66$. Divide by the total number of students: $66 / 20 = 3.3$.

questions.question: A set of numbers has a range of $25$. If every number in the set is multiplied by $3$ and then increased by $10$, what is the range of the new set of numbers?
questions.explanation: Range is the difference between the maximum and minimum values. Multiplying every value by 3 scales the difference (range) by 3: $25 \times 3 = 75$. Adding 10 to every value shifts the entire set but does not change the distance between the maximum and minimum. Thus, the new range is 75.

questions.question:
The table below shows two data sets.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|}\hline \text{Data Set X} & \text{Data Set Y} \\ \hline 5 & 4 \\ \hline 5 & 5 \\ \hline 5 & 5 \\ \hline 5 & 5 \\ \hline 5 & 6 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which of the following must be true about the standard deviations of the two sets?
options: The standard deviation of Set X is zero.
The standard deviation of Set X is greater than Set Y.
The standard deviation of Set Y is zero.
The standard deviations are equal because the means are equal.
questions.explanation: Standard deviation measures the spread of data from the mean. In Set X, every value is identical, meaning there is no spread; therefore, the standard deviation is 0. In Set Y, there is variation (4 and 6), so its standard deviation is greater than 0.

questions.question: A class of 20 students took a test. The 10 students in the morning session had a mean score of $80$. The 10 students in the afternoon session had a mean score of $90$. What was the mean score for the entire class?
questions.explanation: To find the combined mean, calculate the total points for both groups: $(10 \times 80) + (10 \times 90) = 800 + 900 = 1{,}700$. Divide the total points by the total number of students: $1{,}700 / 20 = 85$.

questions.question: A list of 9 numbers is ordered from least to greatest. If the first and last numbers are removed from the list, which of the following measures must remain the same?
questions.explanation: In an ordered list of 9 numbers, the median is the 5th number. Removing the 1st (minimum) and 9th (maximum) leaves 7 numbers. The middle value of these 7 numbers is still the original 5th number. The mean, range, and standard deviation are all sensitive to the removal of extreme values.

questions.question: A data set has a median of $15$ and a mean of $22$. Which of the following best describes the distribution of the data?
options: The distribution is skewed to the left.
The distribution is skewed to the right.
questions.explanation: When the mean is significantly greater than the median, it indicates that the distribution is skewed to the right (positively skewed). This happens because high-value outliers pull the mean upward while the median remains relatively stable.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can confidently calculate weighted means, understand how transformations affect range/SD, and identify distribution skewness from center measures.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on remembering that the median is robust to outliers while the mean is not, and that adding a constant doesn't change range or SD.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: range = max - min, standard deviation measures spread from the mean, and mean > median indicates right skew.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Start by sorting data to find medians, practice calculating weighted means from frequency tables, and visualize how outliers pull the mean.
